coredata.dc:title: Surface passivation by multifunctional carbon dots toward highly efficient and stable inverted perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.

Zhang, Yixin
Pu, Xingyu
Zhao, Junsong
Wang, Tong

Chen, Hui

Zhang, Cheng
Li, Xuanhua
coredata.dc:description: Interfacial imperfections between the perovskite layer and the electron transport layer (ETL) in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) can lead to performance loss and negatively influence long-term operational stability. Here, we introduce an interface engineering method to modify the interface between perovskite and ETL by using multifunctional carbon dots (CDs). C = O in the CDs can chelate with the uncoordinated Pb2+ in the perovskite material, inhibit interfacial recombination, and enhance the performance and stability of device. In addition, –OH in CDs forms hydrogen bonds with I− and organic cation in perovskite, inhibiting light-induced I2 release and organic cation volatilization, causing irreversible degradation of perovskite films, thereby enhancing the long-term operational stability of PSCs. Consequently, we achieve the champion inverted device with an efficiency of 24.02%. The CDs-treated PSCs exhibit high operational stability, and the maximum power point tracking only attenuates by 12.5% after 1000 h. Interfacial modification engineering supported by multifunctional quantum dots can accelerate the road to stable PSCs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interfacial engineering
Carbon dots
Non-radiative recombination

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Molecular interactions. (a) Schematic illustration of the interaction between the CDs and perovskite. (b) TEM image and particle size distribution of the CDs. (c) Absorbance, photoluminescence excitation (emission at 475 nm), and emission spectra under different excitations. (d) FTIR characterization of the CDs with and without PbI2. (e) XPS characterization of Pb element on the perovskite film with and without the CDs. (f) 1H NMR characterization of FAI and FAI-CDs mixtures.

Surface morphology and carrier behavior of perovskite films with 0, 0.08 mg mL−1 CDs. (a, b) Top-view SEM and (c, d) AFM images, the scale bar in the SEM and AFM images is 1 μm. (e) XRD patterns. (f) Time-resolved PL characterization.

Photovoltaic performance. (a) Architecture schematic and cross-sectional SEM of inverted PSCs with structure: ITO/NiO x /PTAA/Al2O3/perovskite film with or without CDs/PC61BM + C60/BCP/Ag. ITO, indiumtin oxide; PTAA, poly[bis (4-phenyl) (2,4,6-trimethylphenyl) amine]; BCP, bathocuproine; PC61BM, [6,6]-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester. (b) J-V characterization of the PSCs treated with different amounts of the CDs. (c) J-V characterization of the PSCs at the different scan directions. (d) Steady-state photocurrent and power outputs of the PSCs. (e) EQE and integrated J SC curves of the PSCs.

Interfacial recombination. (a) UPS characterization. (b) Energy-level scheme for each functional layer. (c) Mott–Schottky plots in of the corresponding PSCs. (d) J–V curves of the ETL-only devices. (e) Dependence of V OC in PSCs on light intensity. (f) Comparison of device FFmea (measured FF) with FFmax and FFS–Q.

Stability characterization and degradation mechanism. (a) Thermal stability of PSCs. (b) Changes of XRD spectra of control and CDs-modified perovskite films against 85 °C. (c) PbI2/perovskite thin film (100) surface peak intensity ratio. (d) Operational stability of encapsulated PSCs under white LED light (100 mW cm−2 in air, 45 °C). (e) The samples were soaked in toluene solution and taken out at 30 h and 60 h to record their UV–Vis spectra. The band at 500 nm corresponds to the absorption peak of I2. (f) Variation of tDOS under 1-sun illumination and bias.

head.ce:title: Surface passivation by multifunctional carbon dots toward highly efficient and stable inverted perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center for Nano Energy Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing, Center for Nano Energy Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi'an 710072, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243032, Anhui, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243032, Anhui, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose a simple interface engineering method to modify the interface between perovskite and ETL by using multifunctional carbon dots (CDs). Interactions between CDs and perovskite layers suppress interfacial recombination and improve device performance; and inhibit photo-induced I2 release and organic cation volatilization causing irreversible degradation of perovskite films, thereby enhancing long-term operational stability of PSCs.

Interfacial imperfections between the perovskite layer and the electron transport layer (ETL) in perovskite solar cells (PSCs) can lead to performance loss and negatively influence long-term operational stability. Here, we introduce an interface engineering method to modify the interface between perovskite and ETL by using multifunctional carbon dots (CDs). C = O in the CDs can chelate with the uncoordinated Pb2+ in the perovskite material, inhibit interfacial recombination, and enhance the performance and stability of device. In addition, –OH in CDs forms hydrogen bonds with I− and organic cation in perovskite, inhibiting light-induced I2 release and organic cation volatilization, causing irreversible degradation of perovskite films, thereby enhancing the long-term operational stability of PSCs. Consequently, we achieve the champion inverted device with an efficiency of 24.02%. The CDs-treated PSCs exhibit high operational stability, and the maximum power point tracking only attenuates by 12.5% after 1000 h. Interfacial modification engineering supported by multifunctional quantum dots can accelerate the road to stable PSCs.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells (PSCs) have begun to be deployed industrially, mainly owing to the outstanding optoelectronic features of perovskite materials [1–3]. Lead halide PSCs have achieved an unparalleled increase in power conversion efficiency (PCE), and the current certified value for inverted single-junction PSCs has reached 25.37% [4]. However, the instability of perovskite materials under operating conditions limits their wide application in optoelectronic devices, mainly due to their ionic nature, weak molecular interactions, and soft lattice characteristics [5,6]. Under severe bias and heat, perovskite materials are prone to induce point defects and ion migration, resulting in the performance degradation of PSCs. In addition to these point and electronic defects, the surface/grain boundaries (GBs) of perovskite films often contain insufficiently coordinated Pb2+ and iodide (I−) ions. Pb2+ is easily reduced to the metal Pb (Pb0), Pb0 is the core of the non-radiative recombination process [7,8]. In addition, I− ions are easily oxidized to the volatile iodine element I2, which will trigger the perovskite radical degradation reaction and accelerate the degradation process [9,10]. Furthermore, vacancies create paths for mobile ions, especially I−, to move to the interface under bias, thereby damaging the charge transport layer and jeopardizing PCE and the long-term operational stability of the PSCs [5,11].

The characterization of traps allows tailoring of molecular modulators through passivation of coordination and ionic bonds [12,13]. Li et al. [14] selected phosphorus (P) atoms in 1,3-bis(diphenylphosphino)propane (DPPP) to passivate lead defects. Zhang et al. [15] suppressed lead defects using nitrogen in pyridine and sulfur in p-toluenethiol. However, these small molecule modifiers can only exhibit a single passivation effect, and have limited effects on the complex characteristics of the perovskite film surface. Passivation of perovskite surfaces with multifunctional molecules would be a potential method to improve device performance and stability. Multifunctional molecules include small molecules [16], carbon dots (CDs) [17], and polymers [18], among others. Due to the insulating properties of most small molecules and polymer modifiers, they cannot greatly promote carrier transport, which limits the degree of device efficiency improvement. The facile synthesis, stability, and low cost of CDs, combined with their semiconducting properties, imply that the development of CDs-based interfacial modifiers has practical advantages both in terms of cost and technology [19].

Here, we modify the interface between the perovskite layer and the electron transport layer (ETL) by using multifunctional CDs (Fig. 1 a). There are abundant chemical interaction sites (C=O) in the CDs, which can chelate with the uncoordinated Pb2+ in the perovskite material, inhibit interfacial recombination, and enhance the performance and stability of device. In addition, –OH in CDs forms hydrogen bonds with I− and FA+ (formamidinium ions) in perovskite, inhibiting light-induced I2 release and organic cation volatilization, causing irreversible degradation of perovskite films, thereby enhancing the long-term operational stability of PSCs. Consequently, we achieve the champion inverted device with a PCE of 24.02%. The CDs-mdofied PSCs exhibit high operational stability, and the maximum power point tracking (MPPT) only attenuates by 12.5% after 1000 h under one sunlight illumination at 45 °C.

We successfully synthesized a blue fluorescent CDs with abundant carboxyl functional groups on the surface by a hydrothermal method (Fig. S1) [20], and demonstrated its structure using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Fig. S2) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. S3). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the as-synthesized CDs had very small particle sizes, with an average size of about 3.5 nm (Fig. 1b). We characterized the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission of CDs (Fig. 1c). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum is divided into two parts, 200–280 nm and 280–400 nm, which correspond to a π → π* transition of the CDs skeleton structure and an n → π* transition of the CDs surface functional groups, respectively [21]. In order to detect the fluorescence properties of CDs, we tested the fluorescence excitation of CDs, and it was found that the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs was at 350 nm, corresponding to the peak of the UV–Vis absorption spectrum at 350 nm. A fluorescence emission test found that CDs showed a strong emission band at 475 nm, a bright blue light under UV light irradiation, and their emission peak position hardly changed under the excitation light varying from 330 to 380 nm, exhibiting excitation-independent stable emission.

FTIR was utilized to identify the change in the chemical environment around the chemical bond, which could demonstrate the interaction between CDs and PbI2 [22,23]. The position of the stretching vibration peak of C=O in CDs is at 1716 cm−1, while the stretching vibration peak of C=O decreased by 15 cm−1 to 1701 cm−1 after the addition of PbI2 (Fig. 1d). The significantly reduced stretch vibration wavenumber means that O in C=O on the surface of CDs acts as an electron donor, and there is a Lewis acid-base interaction with Pb2+ in PbI2, which acts as a passivation effect on Pb2+ defects [24]. In addition, Fig. 1(e) presents the Pb 4f XPS spectrum of the perovskite materials. Compared with the untreated part, after CDs interface treatment, the positions of the Pb 4f 7/2 peak and Pb 4f 5/2 peak shift to the direction of low binding energy by 0.3 eV, indicating that Pb2+ acts as an electron acceptor in the coordination interaction. In addition, the control film shows two smaller peaks at 136.4 and 141.3 eV, which are caused by the presence of metallic lead (Pb0). Pb0 is formed by the reduction of Pb2+ [25]. No Pb0 peaks emerge in the CDs interface modification film, suggesting that the CDs bind to undercoordinated Pb ions that form Pb0, inhibiting its formation. Therefore, it can be proved that C=O in CDs interacts with Pb2+ on the upper surface of the perovskite film, which has the effect of passivating Pb2+ defects.

The presence of hydrogen-bonding interactions between the hydroxyl groups (–OH) of CDs and the perovskite materials was demonstrated using 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR), FTIR, and XPS spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1(f) and Fig. S4, the 1H NMR spectra of FAI and FAI-CDs mixtures in dimethyl sulfoxide-d 6 (CD3SOCD3) solvent were compared. The hydrogen chemical shift (δ) values of the a, b, and c positions of FAI are 9.01, 7.86, 8.65, and 8.68 ppm, while the hydrogen δ values of the a, b positions of FAI in the FAI-CDs mixture do not change, but the hydrogen δ values at the c position change to 8.70 and 8.72 ppm, shifting 0.05 and 0.04 ppm to the high field direction, respectively, indicating that the H atoms of =NH2 + form hydrogen bonds with the O atoms of –OH in CDs, so it can play the role of fixing FA+ in perovskite. In addition, it can also be found from the FTIR that the N–H vibrational peak of the mixture of CDs and FAI has a small shift compared with pure FAI at 3341.6 cm−1, which can also explain the formation of N-H∙∙∙OH hydrogen bonds (Fig. S5) [26]. Moreover, through XPS analysis, the shift of the 3d peak of I− proves that there is a hydrogen force between the –OH of CDs and I− (Fig. S6) [26,27].

The effects of CDs on the surface morphology and roughness of perovskite were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 2 (a and b) presents that the post-treatment of CDs has no obvious effect on the morphology of perovskite. AFM characterization presents that the average roughness (R q) of the control perovskite film is 21.4 nm, while that of the modified CDs is 18.8 nm (Fig. 2c and d). It can be found that the R q reduction of the CDs-modified perovskite film is mainly due to the CDs repairing the surface defects of perovskite films, which contributes to better contact with the ETL (Fig. S7) [28]. The corresponding absorption (Fig. S8) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. 2e) do not change significantly, indicating that the CDs modification occurrs after the crystallization of the perovskite is complete and thus has little effect on the perovskite crystallization.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was used to roughly assess the impact of CDs interface modification on charge carrier behavior [29,30]. The PL peak intensity of the control film is 1.25 × 105, while that of the CDs-modified perovskite film increases to 5.42 × 105 (Fig. S9). It is about 4.3 times higher than that of the reference film, illustrating that the nonradiative recombination of the perovskite film modified by CDs is significantly restrained. The decay process of carriers was studied by time-resolved PL (Fig. 2f and Table S1). The average carrier lifetime increases significantly from 409.79 ns for the reference film to 993.81 ns for the CDs-treated film. The passivation of the perovskite surface by CDs greatly reduces the defect state density on the perovskite surface, thus reducing the trapping of photogenerated carriers by point defects on the perovskite surface, thereby prolonging the carrier lifetime, which positively contributes to device efficiency [31].

We investigated the influence of CDs on the photovoltaic performance of the device using inverted planar heterojunction structured PSCs. A schematic diagram of the device structure and a cross-sectional SEM are shown in Fig. 3 (a). Fig. S10 and Table S2 count the effects of different amounts of CDs on the photoelectric parameters of the device. The average PCE of control devices is 20.98% ± 0.77%, and the PCEs of devices modified with different concentrations of CDs are 22.06% ± 0.50% (0.05 mg mL−1), 22.88% ± 0.39% (0.08 mg mL−1), and 22.12% ± 0.59% (0.1 mg mL−1). Compared to control, the devices modified with the optimal concentration of CDs have the smallest standard deviation, showing excellent reproducibility. The J-V (current density-voltage) characteristic curves of the highest PCE of PSCs modified with different concentrations of CDs are shown in Fig. 3(b), and the optimal number of CDs-modified PSCs is confirmed to be 0.08 mg mL−1. Fig. 3(c) measures the forward and reverse scans of the reference and optimal concentration CDs-treated PSCs. The highest PCEs of the control in the forward and reverse scans reach 21.22% and 22.04%, respectively. While the device modified with the optimal concentration of CDs in forward and reverse scans is 23.46% and 24.02%, respectively. The hysteresis factor (HF) is quantified as
(1)
HF =
PCE
reverse
-
PCE
forward
PCE
reverse

The HF of control is 3.72%, while the HF of CDs-modified device is 2.33%. We use electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to explain the reason for the reduction of HF in CDs-modified devices (Fig. S11). Compared with the control, the charge transfer resistance (R ct) of the CDs-modified device decreases, indicating that the charge transfer of the perovskite/ETL becomes more efficient, while the increased recombination resistance (R rec) indicates that the passivation of CDs on the perovskite surface reduces charge carrier recombination at the interface. The results indicate that CDs simultaneously act as defect passivation and facilitate charge transport, contributing to lower device hysteresis. Fig. 3(d) exhibits the steady-state power output (SPO) and current density output curves. The SPO for devices is stable at 21.24% and 23.77%, respectively, which is in line with the J-V curve findings. The integrated short-circuit current densities (J SC) of the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra devices without or with CDs interface treatment are 23.63 and 24.28 mA cm−2 (Fig. 3e), which deviate from the J SC values obtained from the J-V curves by less than 5%, which is considered to be normal performance.

The electronic structure of perovskite films was studied employing ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS, Fig. 4 a) and bandgap (E g = 1.54 eV, Fig. S12) before and after CDs modification [32,33]. Compared with the untreated part, the Fermi level (E F) of the CDs-modified perovskite film shifts upward by 0.18 eV, while the conduction band (CB) shifts upward by only 0.03 eV (Fig. 4b), illustrating that the surface of the perovskite film modified by CDs exhibits more n-type characteristics, mainly because CDs repair the electron-deficiency defects on the perovskite surface, forming a back surface field with the bulk phase of the perovskite, which is in the same direction as the built-in electric field of the device, so that the built-in electric field of the CDs-treated device becomes stronger (1.07 V versus 1.12 V, Fig. 4c) [34], which promotes electron transport and suppresses hole transport and ultimately improves the open-circuit voltage (V OC) of PSCs [1].

We fabricated ETL-only devices (ITO/SnO2/perovskite film/PC61BM + C60/Ag) and evaluated the effect of CDs on defect state density and carrier mobility using the space charge limited current (SCLC) method [35,36]. The low-bias region is the ohmic region, the middle-bias region is the defect filling limit region, and the high-bias region is the SCLC region (Fig. 4d). The defect filling limit voltage (V TFL) is determined at the junction of the low and middle bias regions, and the defect density (N t) is obtained using Eq. (2) [37].
(2)
N
t
=
2 ε
ε
0
V
TFL
e
L
2

where ε 0 and ε are the vacuum and relative permittivity, e is the electron charge, and L is the film thickness. The V TFL value of the control is 0.66 V, and the calculated N t is 2.90 × 1015 cm−3, while the V TFL of the CDs-modified device is 0.48 V, and the calculated N t is 2.11 × 1015 cm−3 (Fig. 4d). The significant reduction in the defect density of CDs interface treated devices is primarily due to the interface passivation of CDs.

According to the Mott-Gurney law in Eq. (3), the carrier mobility (μ) can be calculated from the SCLC region [38].
(3)
μ =
8 9
J
ε
ε 0
L
3
V
2

The μ of the control device is 4.11 cm2 V−1 s−1, and the μ of the CDs-modified device is as high as 16.01 cm2 V−1 s−1. The increase in carrier mobility of CDs-modified devices is mainly due to the elimination of interface traps caused by CDs and the excellent conductivity of CDs at the interface [19,39,40].

The carrier recombination mechanism was determined by studying the dependence of V OC of PSCs on the incident light intensity (I) using Eq. (4): [41].
(4)
V
O C
=
n
k
B
T
q
ln
I
where n is the ideality factor, k B is the Boltzmann constant, and T is the temperature. The n value for the CDs-treated device reduces from 1.49 (reference) to 1.15, manifesting that defect-induced carrier recombination is reduced [42]. Quantitative analysis of enhanced FF (from 80.21% to 83.55%) in CDs-modified devices using an n-value. The E g of the perovskite film is 1.54 eV, and its thermodynamic limit FF (FFS-Q) is 90.10% [43]. The maximum FF (FFmax) can be estimated by Eq. (5) [44].
(5)
FF
max
=
v
oc
- ln
v
oc
+ 0.72
v
oc
+ 1
w i t h
v
oc
=
q
V
oc
n
k B
T

The estimated FFmax for the control is 85.40%, while it is 88.18% for the CDs-modified device. (Fig. 4f). The interfacial defect passivation of CDs significantly improves the FF of the device due to the suppression of nonradiative recombination (4.70% versus 1.92%) and reduces charge transport loss (5.19% versus 4.63%). This FF analysis highlights the excellent properties of CDs that overcome the V OC-FF trade-off.

Thermal stability of PSCs is also a thorny issue to be addressed [45–47]. The unencapsulated PSCs were placed in a N2 environment for 85 °C heating and aging, and the normalized PCE changes of the control and the CDs-modified device were recorded, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). Compared with their initial PCE after 1000 h, the CDs-modified device retains 87%, while the control device retains only 39%. To explore the reason why the CDs interface treatment improves the device's stability, the perovskite films without or with CDs treatment were aged at 85 °C in a N2 environment (Fig. 5b). The relative intensity ratio between the perovskite (100) peak and the PbI2 peak is used to indicate the degree of decomposition of the perovskite material [48]. After heating for 100 h, the PbI2/(100) peak ratio of the control is 0.76, while that of the CDs-modified device is 0.6 (Fig. 5c). After aging for 300 h, the PbI2 peak intensity of the control has far exceeded the (100) peak, indicating that the perovskite film has been significantly decomposed. However, the intensity of the PbI2 peak of the CDs-treated device is still lower than that of the (100) peak, indicating that the CDs modification has a significant effect on the stability of the perovskite film at high temperatures. The main reason is that the hydrogen bonding between CDs and organic cations prevents the volatilization of organic cations in perovskite materials.

MPPT technology was the most practical method for the stability test of PSCs, so the MPPT test was used to monitor the change in SPO of the encapsulated device under the conditions of 45 °C and one-sun illumination (Fig. 5d) [49]. After 450 h of continuous operation of the control device, the SPO value is only 61.7% compared with the initial value. In contrast, the CDs-modified device maintains 87.5% of its initial SPO after 1000 h of continuous operation. To understand the mechanism of enhanced stability, we performed analyses of aged perovskite films and devices. I− ions are known to migrate under high-temperature operating conditions, degrading device performance. I2 is one of the key factors in the degradation of perovskite films under illumination [9,50,51]. I− traps holes to become neutral I, and two neutral interstitial I atoms migrate and combine to form I2. This process is more likely to take place on the perovskite surface due to its higher defect density than the bulk. To verify this, we immersed the perovskite film in toluene at 45 °C under sunlight irradiation and studied the formation of I2 by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 5e). Toluene and I2 form a compound with an absorption peak at 500 nm [52]. We observe that the control film releases I2 quicker than the CDs-modified film. These results suggest that the interfacial passivation of CDs greatly reduces ion migration and suppresses I2 formation. Fig. 5(f) shows the variation in the trap density of states (tDOS) of device under illumination and bias voltage. For calculation details, refer to the Supporting Information. Compared with the reference, the tDOS of the CDs-treated PSC does not change obviously after running for 500 h, which proves that the CDs inhibit the progress of perovskite decomposition promoted by light and bias. Of course, there are multiple carbonyls (C=O) in CDs, which form strong chelation interactions with uncoordinated lead in perovskite materials to suppress lead defects. Furthermore, –OH in CDs forms hydrogen bonds with organic cations (FA+) and halogen anions (I−) in perovskite to prevent ion migration (Fig. S13). The CDs on the perovskite surface prevent the decomposition of the perovskite material under light and bias conditions, contributing to the stability of the PSCs.
In conclusion, multifunctional CDs modify the interface between perovskite and ETL.

There are abundant chemical interaction sites (C=O) in the CDs, which can chelate with the uncoordinated lead in the perovskite material, inhibit the interfacial recombination, and improve the performance and stability of device. In addition, –OH in CDs forms hydrogen bonds with I− and FA+ in perovskite, inhibiting light-induced I2 release and organic cation volatilization, causing irreversible degradation of perovskite films, thereby enhancing the long-term operational stability of PSCs. Consequently, we achieve the champion inverted device with a PCE of 24.02%. The CDs-treated PSCs exhibit high operational stability, and the MPPT only attenuates by 12.5% after 1000 h. Interfacial modification engineering supported by multifunctional quantum dots can accelerate the road to stable PSCs.
Experimental details can be found in the Supporting Information.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172237, 22261142666), the Shaanxi International Cooperation Project (2020KWZ-018), the Shaanxi Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars (2022JC-21), the Research Fund of the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing (NPU), China (2021-QZ-02), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (D5000220033), and the Hong Kong Scholars Program (XJ2022025).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2023.07.002.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
